title: Scalable hybrid online machine learning for real-time intrusion detection in electric vehicle charging systems
abstract: Electric Vehicle Charging Systems (EVCS) are increasingly integrated into smart grids, but their openness and connectivity expose them to diverse cyber threats. Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) are essential for securing these infrastructures; however, conventional offline models struggle with real-time data streams, evolving attack patterns, and concept drift. This study investigates online machine learning for real-time IDS in EVCS. Two approaches are evaluated: a baseline Adaptive Random Forest (ARF) with ADWIN drift detection, and a scalable hybrid ensemble integrating ARF with Naïve Bayes, k-Nearest Neighbors, and Logistic Regression under a meta-learning framework. Experimental results show that while the baseline ARF achieves competitive performance, it incurs high computational cost (267.98 minutes for the 15-class task). In contrast, the hybrid model reduces runtime to 81.65 minutes while improving accuracy (0.8582 vs. 0.8142), precision (0.8796 vs. 0.8341), recall (0.8582 vs. 0.8142), and F1-score (0.8344 vs. 0.8002). Notably, in binary classification, the hybrid ensemble achieves near-perfect detection (F1 = 1.0000) while maintaining stability under drift-free conditions. These findings demonstrate that combining diverse classifiers enhances drift resilience, improves computational efficiency, and strengthens real-time detection, offering a scalable IDS solution tailored to EVCS and advancing the security and reliability of modern smart grids.
text:
Date of publication xxxx 00, 0000, date of current version xxxx 00, 0000.
Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2023.0322000
Scalable Hybrid Online Machine Learning for
Real-Time Intrusion Detection in Electric Vehicle
Charging Systems
JOSIAH KIPRUTO1, BERNARDI PRANGGONO1 (Senior Member, IEEE), and RAJ SHUKLA1,
1School of Computing and Information Science, Anglia Ruskin University, Cambridge CB1 1PT, UK
Corresponding author: Bernardi Pranggono (e-mail: bernardi.pranggono@aru.ac.uk)
ABSTRACT Electric Vehicle Charging Systems (EVCS) are increasingly integrated into smart grids, but
their openness and connectivity expose them to diverse cyber threats. Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) are
essential for securing these infrastructures; however, conventional offline models struggle with real-time data
streams, evolving attack patterns, and concept drift. This study investigates online machine learning for real-
time IDS in EVCS. Two approaches are evaluated: a baseline Adaptive Random Forest (ARF) with ADWIN
drift detection, and a scalable hybrid ensemble integrating ARF with Naïve Bayes, k-Nearest Neighbors, and
Logistic Regression under a meta-learning framework. Experimental results show that while the baseline
ARF achieves competitive performance, it incurs high computational cost (267.98 minutes for the 15-class
task). In contrast, the hybrid model reduces runtime to 81.65 minutes while improving accuracy (0.8582 vs.
0.8142), precision (0.8796 vs. 0.8341), recall (0.8582 vs. 0.8142), and F1-score (0.8344 vs. 0.8002). Notably,
in binary classification, the hybrid ensemble achieves near-perfect detection (F1 = 1.0000) while maintaining
stability under drift-free conditions. These findings demonstrate that combining diverse classifiers enhances
drift resilience, improves computational efficiency, and strengthens real-time detection, offering a scalable
IDS solution tailored to EVCS and advancing the security and reliability of modern smart grids.
INDEX TERMS Electric Vehicle Charging Systems (EVCS), Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), Online
Machine Learning, Hybrid Ensemble Models, Concept Drift, Smart Grid Cybersecurity
I. INTRODUCTION
The global shift towards electric mobility has resulted in sub-
stantial expansion in the adoption of electric vehicles (EVs),
driven by the imperative to reduce carbon emissions, evolving
energy policies, and advancements in technological innova-
tion. This transition is accompanied by the rapid development
of infrastructure systems, particularly Electric Vehicle Charg-
ing Systems (EVCS) (see Figure 1). According to research,
the global EVCS industry is projected to expand from USD
15.57 billion in 2024 to USD 96.88 billion by 2032 [1]. The
growth of the EV sector necessitates the implementation of
robust, scalable, and secure systems, encompassing real-time
communication, billing, and vehicle-grid integration func-
tionalities [2].
Standardized communication protocols were developed to
achieve interoperability between various stakeholders in the
EV ecosystem (EVs, charging stations, and backend servers).
The two protocols mainly used are Open Charge Point Proto-
col (OCPP) and ISO 15118, which provide key services (e.g.,
plug-and-charge, secure authentication, firmware updates,
and remote monitoring) [3]. Although these protocols enable
functionalities and integration of systems, they also create
vulnerabilities for cyber attackers to take advantage of. The
open and generally weakly secured communications channels
open EVCS networks up to vulnerabilities like spoofing, port
scans, SYN floods, and denial-of-service (DoS) attacks [4].
Cyber intrusions put user privacy and safety at risk, compro-
mising the operational security of EV infrastructure and the
larger smart grid [5].
This study advances the state of the art in EVCS cyber-
security by introducing a scalable online machine learning
framework for real-time intrusion detection. The main con-
tributions and their significance are summarized as follows:
• Drift-sensitive hybrid architecture: We propose a novel
IDS framework that combines ARF with meta-learning,
enabling dynamic adaptation to evolving attack patterns
and concept drift in EVCS environments.
• Optimized base learner integration: By aggregating

FIGURE 1: EVCS Ecosystems
Naïve Bayes, k-Nearest Neighbors, and Logistic Regres-
sion within the ensemble, the system balances detection
accuracy, robustness, and computational efficiency.
• Efficiency without performance loss: We demonstrate
that training time can be significantly reduced without
degrading detection performance, a critical requirement
for real-time deployment.
• Modular streaming pipeline: The study introduces a re-
peatable and extensible pipeline for online intrusion de-
tection, which can be transferred to other cyber-physical
systems requiring real-time security.
• Comprehensive validation: The model is empirically
validated on the dataset across both binary and multi-
class intrusion detection tasks, addressing a gap in IDS
research and reinforcing its practical applicability.
These contributions collectively advance the state-of-the-
art in adaptive cybersecurity for smart grid-integrated EV
infrastructure and provide a foundation for future research in
scalable, intelligent IDS solutions.
The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: Sec-
tion II reviews related work on intrusion detection systems
for EVCS. Section III describes the dataset, preprocessing
methods, and experimental setup. Section IV presents the
results and comparative analysis. Section V discusses the
findings and their implications, and Section VI concludes the
paper with recommendations for future research.
II. RELATED WORK
Static or offline machine learning methods have been ex-
tensively applied in the development of intrusion detection
systems (IDS). These models are typically trained on a pre-
determined dataset prior to deployment and remain con-
stant during their operational phase. Conventional algorithms,
such as decision trees, support vector machines (SVMs), and
random forests, have demonstrated adequate performance
in general network intrusion detection tasks. Within EVCS
environments, these models have been utilized to identify
known threats through the analysis of preprocessed traffic
logs. Nevertheless, these models presuppose a stationary data
distribution, wherein normal and malicious traffic patterns
remain unchanged over time. This assumption is invalid in
actual EVCS networks, where traffic dynamics are influenced
by firmware updates, the introduction of new EV models,
backend server upgrades, and variations in user behavior
[6]. Consequently, static models become obsolete—or less
effective—over time. This obsolescence necessitates periodic
retraining, which is resource-intensive and impractical for
implementation in real-time applications.
Online machine learning is an area of machine learning
where the algorithm processes data continuously, one in-
stance at a time or in small batches, instead of training on
the data set all at once. Online learning is distinct from batch
learning in that a batch learning model must be retrained when
new data is acquired. In contrast, an online learner processes
the newest data instance, essential for streaming data [4]. This
is especially important in situations such as EVCS, where the
network traffic evolves rapidly, and new attack patterns may
develop unpredictably. Online learning allows for adaptive
actions as events occur, and IDS can operate successfully
in real-time, even under non-stationary data distribution and
unknown threats.
One of the key advantages of online machine learning in
EVCS is the ability to deliver low-latency updates with rela-
tively little computational cost [7]. EVCS is also distributed
over vast geographic areas, and cyber anomalies will need
real-time decisions made to avoid interruptions. Static models
may require whole-scale retraining on enormous datasets,
which could lead to delays in detection and response. Online
learners can process a single new data point nearly instanta-
neously, which makes them a good fit for detecting anomalies
in real-time in EVCS traffic. Additionally, a significant ad-
vantage of online learning systems is that they are memory-
efficient, which is essential to consider when employing
resource-limited edge devices that will ultimately be part of
the charging station.
Several online learning algorithms have been created and
utilized successfully in IDS. One of the first algorithms is
the Hoeffding Tree, or Very Fast Decision Tree (VFDT).
It can construct a decision tree incrementally and applies
continual checking of the Hoeffding bound to determine at
any point that there is sufficient evidence to split the nodes,
and hence is fast on large data streams. The ARF ensemble
method also comprises Hoeffding Trees, which contribute
to further classification accuracy. ARF is more resilient due
to the application of drift detectors per tree in conjunction
with a simple majority voting of the ensemble, rendering the
method particularly useful in high-dimensional and dynamic
settings of intrusion detection [8]. Other types of ensembling
(Online Bagging and Online Boosting) apply conventional
ideas of bagging and boosting to enhance generalization in
2

XXX et al.: Scalable Hybrid Online Machine Learning for Real-Time Intrusion Detection in EVCS
the situation of online learning.
A significant problem that has been addressed by online
learning in IDS is the issue of concept drift (the idea that
the statistical properties of the target variable change over
time). In EVCS, there could be a drift from updated commu-
nication protocols, new additions of EV models, or updates
to adaptive attack characteristics. Online IDS approaches
typically include drift detection mechanisms that monitor the
learning algorithm’s performance and notify the algorithm
that model adaption is required. ADWIN is a widely used
drift detection method, which keeps a sliding window of
variable size and uses statistical tests to observe changes in
data distribution, updating the model or retraining based on
the new distribution when drift is detected [9]. The Drift
Detection Method (DDM) monitors error rates and raises an
alarm when a statistically significant increase occurs. The
Early Drift Detection Method (EDDM) extends DDM by
looking at the distance between classification errors and is
more applicable to gradual drifts.
The existing context shows that online ML offers some
promise for IDS across different contexts, including smart
grids, industrial control systems, and vehicular networks. For
instance, Almania et al. implemented online random forest
classifiers on network traffic and noted improvements in
detection accuracy and drift adaptation [10].
The development of advanced and adaptive IDS for de-
ployment in EVCS has highlighted the issues associated with
static, classic classifier systems. As threats evolve and data
streams become increasingly variable, models can no longer
rely on one-off classifications; they must also adapt to con-
cept drift. Although DL approaches have made significant
progress in offline environments, producing a model that
requires tremendous processing power and cannot account for
changes in a real-time, resource-depleted environment [11].
As a result, hybrid ensemble learning methods have started to
receive attention for providing flexibility with performance in
streaming data scenarios.
Existing work has extensively confirmed one of the bene-
fits of the hybrid ensemble learners compared to one-model
learners in situations of streaming and dynamic data. For
instance, Montiel et al. confirmed that combining NB and
tree-based learners substantially improved accuracy and re-
action time when there is drift in online learning situations
[12]. Bifet and Gavalda were among the first authors who
highlighted the importance of adaptive learning mechanisms
in data streams through their work on Adaptive Sliding Win-
dows, offering the origins of dynamic drift detection in real-
time systems [13].
Empirical evaluation using the CICEVSE2024 dataset re-
vealed improved performance across all streaming metrics,
including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. This was
especially evident in the 15-class classification task, where
the hybrid model outperformed the baseline ARF setup. Ad-
ditionally, execution time was significantly reduced, making
the hybrid solution more practical for real-time deployment
in EVCS. Environments. These results support earlier claims
in the literature and demonstrate that hybrid models (when
thoughtfully integrated with meta-learning and drift-aware
mechanisms) offer not only predictive accuracy but also com-
putational efficiency under evolving network conditions.
Despite progress in online learning for IDS, research re-
mains limited in applying hybrid ensemble models to real-
time cybersecurity, particularly within EVCS. Most studies
have focused on traditional ensemble models like ARF with-
out integrating meta-learning for adaptive prediction weight-
ing. Additionally, while concept drift is a known challenge in
streaming environments, many approaches lack the integra-
tion of dynamic drift detection mechanisms such as ADWIN.
This omission restricts the model’s ability to maintain high
accuracy under evolving network conditions. Furthermore,
execution efficiency and real-time deployment feasibility are
rarely addressed, even though they are critical for applications
in fast-response domains like EVCS.
Another significant gap lies in the underutilization of cu-
mulative metric monitoring alongside online learning. Few
models track performance metrics such as accuracy, pre-
cision, recall, and F1-score over time, which are essential
for evaluating adaptability in streaming data scenarios. The
existing literature also lacks empirical comparisons between
baseline ARF models and more advanced hybrid approaches
using the same dataset for binary and multiclass tasks. As
such, there is limited evidence on the superiority of hybrid
models in identical experimental settings. These gaps high-
light the need for a flexible, drift-aware, and performance-
monitored hybrid IDS model, which is addressed in this study.
III. METHODOLOGY
In this section, we explain the the methodology adopted in
this research.
A. DATASET DESCRIPTION AND PREPROCESSING
The CICEVSE2024 dataset [14], a formally defined intrusion
detection dataset designed explicitly for EVCS, was utilized
in this work. The dataset consisted of tabular records, where
each row of instances contained a collection of engineered
attributes, such as packet size, flow length, and protocol type,
among others, and related labels representing the type of traf-
fic. Two labelling schemes were employed: a 2-class binary
classification (Benign vs. Attack) and a 15-class multiclass
classification, reflecting different types of attacks, such as
DoS, spoofing, and ransomware.
Before streaming analysis could be applied, some pre-
processing had taken place. This encompassed handling of
null values, one-hot encoding of the categorical values, and
normalisation of numerical ranges of feature values. All of
this helped make the data amenable to the algorithms used
in online learning, which work incrementally on data. The
Hoeffding Tree’s feature importance analysis capability can
facilitate utilization of feature priority based on information
gain for quick and effective feature selection []. To simulate
practical traffic, the work also converted the dataset into a
stream format, which enables handling of online learning and

FIGURE 2: Hybrid Model Architecture
drift detection (an important necessity of real-time intrusion
detection). Such clean stream data would compare equally
well, allowing a comparison of both baseline and hybrid
performance, objectively and fairly determining optimal per-
formance in a dynamic network and live environment.
B. HYBRID MODEL DESIGN
The hybrid framework had evolved into enhanced streaming
classification platforms that utilized various learners in a
mixed architecture of drift-aware ensembles. The foundation
of the architecture was an ARF model, recognized for its
practical application in processing dynamic data streams, as
well as its in-built conceptual drift detectors. This base was
placed on top of a set of dynamic base learners, including
KNN, NB, and LR. All of the base learners were written using
libraries that can be updated over the net, and therefore can
exist non-destructively with the data stream.
A meta-learning structure was constructed that would align
the decisions of the ensemble. This module is based on con-
tinuously approximating the performance of base learners on
new data, using a weighting scheme that assigns likelihoods
to predictions using new statistics of accuracy or confidence.
Such weighting was applied to a confidence-weighted voting
process, whereby predictions would be a function of the
relative reliabilities of each learner within the current concept
window. In situations where performance breakdowns were
detected, the meta-learner would trigger the replacement or
retraining of inadequately performing models based on drift
detection feedback supplied by the base ARF components.
Fig. 2 illustrates the architecture of the proposed hybrid
intrusion detection model developed in this study. It shows
how streaming data is processed through preprocessing, drift
detection, multiple base learners, and a meta-learning layer to
generate adaptive and accurate real-time predictions.
C. EXPERIMENTAL SETUP
All experiments were conducted on Google Colab Pro (70GB
of RAM), providing sufficient computational power for real-
time stream processing and model training. Necessary li-
braries included River for online learning, Scikit-learn for
base learner integration, and Pandas for data handling [15]–
[18].
A simulated data stream was created from the preprocessed
CICEVSE2024 dataset to mimic the real-time arrival of data.
Instances were fed sequentially to the models using fixed time
intervals, preserving the original order of records to maintain
the temporal structure critical for drift detection and stream-
ing evaluation. Both binary (2-class) and multiclass (15-class)
classification experiments were conducted in separate runs,
ensuring consistent benchmarking across both scenarios.
The evaluation procedure was designed to capture key per-
formance aspects. Real-time tracking of accuracy, F1-score,
recall, and precision was enabled via cumulative metric mon-
itoring. Concept drift checkpoints were integrated to observe
model adaptability over time. Execution time was automati-
cally recorded for each classification scenario to assist with an
efficiency analysis. Random seeds were frozen when initial-
izing the models to ensure reproducibility. All configuration
files, model versions, and experiment scripts were version-
controlled with Git. The test conditions, including the batch
size, the order of streams presented to the model, and all
model parameters, remained consistent across replications
to provide a valid comparative analysis of the baseline and
hybrid approaches. This methodological framework enabled
repeatable measurements of the performance, robustness, and
scalability of the proposed method.
D. EVALUATION METRICS AND MONITORING
The model’s performance was monitored through four key
real-time metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score.
This selective choice of metrics was due to their direct appli-
cability in estimating classification performance, particularly
in streaming intrusion detection situations where class im-
balance and drift are known to affect outcomes significantly.
Accuracy estimated the general correctness of the predic-
tion, while Precision and Recall provided information on the
model’s capability to detect the minority class (attack). F1-
Score, which is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall,
offered a balanced estimation, which is of particular signifi-
cance in situations of different data distributions.
To monitor such metrics effectively throughout the entire
data stream, we employed a cumulative tracking system via
River’s built-in metrics module. Performance metrics were
computed at each time step using a sliding window func-
tionality, which facilitated real-time smoothing of trends and
prevented abrupt jumps in metrics due to transient drift or
noise. This window-based system of monitoring facilitated
continuous observation of model performance during variable
data fragments and allowed for a response to declines in
performance.
Matplotlib was used to visualize the results of the metric,
allowing the development of the metric over time to be ob-
served [19]. This visualization helped verify the workability
of the drift handling and adaptive learning components, both
in binary and multiclass scenarios, presenting clear evidence

of real-time IDS activity within the temporal window of

E. VISUALIZATION AND INTERPRETABILITY
To make the models more comprehensible and easier to
interpret, and to facilitate better estimation of their perfor-
mance, various visualization tools were utilized during the
experiments. Visualization of measures, such as cumulative
accuracy and F1-score over time, enabled tracking of the
models’ learning in real-time. Similarly, visualizations of drift
showed the identified points of drift and model resets, helping
to understand the adaptive learning aspect of the learners.
Feature importance visualization was achieved by observing
the decision path of Hoeffding Trees [20], alongside the accu-
mulated feature value of the top N contributing features over
time. Visualization helped interpret how decisions were made
and which features contributed the most to the prediction,
ultimately leading to a better, more transparent, and more
interpretable IDS.
F. PERFORMANCE METRICS
To effectively assess the performance of an ML model, it
is crucial to employ an appropriate metric that reflects the
accuracy and reliability of the model. For binary classifica-
tion tasks, such as distinguishing between two outcomes, the
confusion matrix is a commonly used, straightforward metric.
This matrix provides four distinct prediction outcomes: (i)
True Positive, where a fraudulent transaction is accurately
identified as fraud; (ii) False Positive, where a legitimate
transaction is mistakenly labeled as fraud; (iii) False Neg-
ative, where a fraudulent transaction is incorrectly marked
as legitimate; and (iv) True Negative, where a legitimate
transaction is correctly classified as such. A multi-pronged
metric approach was employed to ascertain model perfor-
mance, utilizing the validation dataset:
• Accuracy reflects the proportion of correct predictions
made.
• Precision quantifies the accuracy of positive predictions.
• Recall highlights the fraction of positives that were
rightly classified.
• F1-Score offers a harmonic balance between precision
and recall, particularly crucial when faced with class
imbalances.
IV. EVALUATION AND RESULTS
In this section, the performance metrics of the proposed sys-
tem were evaluated.
A. PROPOSED HYBRID MODEL (HT + NB + LR + KNN
WITH ADWIN)
15-Class Problem
The proposed hybrid model was evaluated on the 15-
class problem, and the results demonstrate competitive per-
formance with effective adaptation to concept drifts. A total
of 17 drift events were detected across the stream at varying

intervals, beginning as early as instance 480 (0.02 minutes)
and continuing up to instance 852,064 (54.54 minutes). This
indicates the sensitivity of the model to evolving data distri-
butions, particularly in the early stages of stream processing.
The hybrid model achieved a final accuracy of 0.8582,
with a precision of 0.8796, recall of 0.8582, and an F1-score
of 0.8344, confirming its ability to balance predictive per-
formance across multiple classes. Importantly, the execution
time was reduced to 81.65 minutes, which is significantly
more efficient than the baseline model, highlighting the com-
putational benefits of the hybrid approach.
Figures 3 to 9 illustrate the evaluation metrics over time,
drift detection events, class distribution dynamics, and feature
importance. The metrics plots (Figures 3 and 4) show that
accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score improved gradu-
ally after initial drifts, stabilizing around higher values. The
processing time distribution (Figure 5) indicates consistent
runtime efficiency, with no significant outliers detected. Drift
and metrics contributions are visualized in Figure 6, con-
firming the interplay between adaptation and performance
maintenance. Furthermore, Figure 7 highlights accuracy vari-
ations before and after drift events, showing that the model
consistently recovered performance after drift adaptation.
The class distribution plot (Figure 8) reveals the imbal-
ance inherent in the 15-class dataset, with some classes
dominating the stream. Finally, feature importance (Figure
9) highlights the most influential predictors, with variables
such as dst2sr_bytes, bidirectional_max_ps, and applica-
tion_is_guessed emerging as key drivers of classification per-
formance.
Overall, the results indicate that the hybrid model offers a

2-Class Problem
The hybrid model achieved exceptional performance in
the binary classification problem. Unlike the 15-class case,
no concept drifts were detected throughout the entire data
stream, indicating the robustness and stability of the model to
FIGURE 6: Cumulative contributions of drift detection and
metrics evaluation with total time
FIGURE 7: Pre- and post-drift accuracy comparison across
17 drift events (15-class)
distinguish between benign and attack traffic. This stability
translated into near-perfect metrics, with a final accuracy of
0.9999, precision of 0.9999, recall of 1.0000, and an F1-score
of 1.0000.
In addition to outstanding predictive performance, the
model completed execution in 70.92 minutes, a significant
improvement in computational efficiency compared to both
the baseline and the 15-class hybrid experiment. Overall,
the hybrid model shows good generalizability in the binary
condition and is more accurate and less sensitive to drift.

B. COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
To assess the effectiveness of the proposed hybrid model,
we conducted a comparative analysis against the baseline
approach [7] across both the 15-class and 2-class problems.
The comparison considered execution time, final accuracy,
precision, recall, F1-score, and the number of drift detections.
Key Improvements Observed:
FIGURE 12: Processing time distribution for 2-class hybrid

under hybrid model
• Faster Execution: The hybrid model significantly de-
creased training and evaluation time. For the 15-class
issue, execution time decreased from 267.98 minutes to
81.65 minutes, which is an improvement of nearly 70%.
Similarly, for the 2-class problem, the execution time
was decreased from 106.54 minutes to 70.92 minutes.
• Better Accuracy and Stability: The hybrid method ex-
ceeded the baseline method in both of the tasks, as
evidenced by improved accuracy, precision, recall, and
F1-scores. This was especially seen in the 2-class case,
in which the hybrid model achieved close to perfect
classification performance.
• Reduced Drift Events: The hybrid model showed im-
proved adaptability to concept drift, with drift detections
dropping from 36 to 17 in the 15-class task, and 0 drifts
in the 2-class problem. Overall, this shows the hybrid
model is more stable and can withstand evolving data
FIGURE 15: Feature importance ranking for 2-class hybrid
model
distributions.
The comparison results demonstrated the effectiveness,
precision, and reliability of the hybrid model as a more ef-
ficient means for real-time intrusion detection in contrast to
the baseline method.
V. CONCLUSION
The overarching goal of this study was to enhance real-time
intrusion detection capabilities in EVCS. Specifically, this
study aimed to investigate the feasibility of applying and
exploiting adaptive online learning methods given streaming
data related to cybersecurity. The research followed a two-
phase methodology. The first involved replicating a baseline
model using ARF based on prior literature to establish a
reliable performance benchmark. The second phase focused
on developing a novel hybrid model that combined ARF
with dynamic base learners and a meta-learning strategy to
enhance decision-making under drift and class imbalance

conditions.
Throughout the project, emphasis was placed on the chal-
lenges of concept drift and the demands of real-time pro-
cessing. To model realistic EVCS world environment simu-
lations, data was streamed incrementally without batch re-
training with updates from within the model’s perspective.
It was evaluated against two tasks: a low-complexity binary
classification task (benign or attack) and a higher-complexity
15-class multiclass task mirroring network attack categories.
This two-task evaluation methodology provided informative
insights into model behaviour, allowing us to analyse model
behaviour across a suite of classification complexity and drift
scenarios. We did not conduct our research upon offline static
evaluations; we wished to conduct more realistic streamed-
based studies that were indicative of the deployed operating
context within EVCS operational systems.

JOSIAH KIPRUTO received an M.Sc. in Cyber
Security with Distinction from Anglia Ruskin Uni-
versity, Cambridge, UK. His research interests in-
clude cybersecurity, critical infrastructure, and AI.
BERNARDI PRANGGONO
(Senior Member,
IEEE) is an Associate Professor in Cyber Security
and Computer Networks at the School of Comput-
ing and Information Science, Anglia Ruskin Uni-
versity, Cambridge, UK. Dr. Pranggono received
his B.Eng. degree in Electronics and Telecom-
munication Engineering from Waseda University,
Japan, an M.DigComms degree in Digital Commu-
nications from Monash University, Australia, and
a Ph.D. degree in Electronics and Electrical Engi-
neering from the University of Leeds, UK. He has previously held academic
and research positions at Sheffield Hallam University, Glasgow Caledonian
University, Queen’s University Belfast, and the University of Leeds. He has
held industrial positions at Pricewaterhouse Coopers, Accenture, and Telstra.
His current research interests include cybersecurity, the Internet of Things,
AI, and green ICT.
RAJ MANI SHUKLA is a Senior Lecturer ( Assis-
tant Professor) at Anglia Ruskin University, Cam-
bridge, UK. His research intersects with AI, the
Internet of Things, and cybersecurity. Some of the
research topics that Raj worked on include: trust-
worthy and responsible AI, privacy-preserving AI,
sustainable cities, healthcare data analytics, cyber-
security, and cloud/edge computing. Before join-
ing ARU, Raj worked as a KTP Associate - Data
Scientist at the University of Bristol. He completed
his Ph.D. in Computer Science and Engineering at the University of Nevada,
Reno, under the supervision of Dr. Shamik Sengupta. Raj worked as a Junior
Research Fellow (JRF) at IIT Kanpur. He received master’s degree from NIT
Kurukshetra and completed B.Tech from BIET Jhansi.
